title: Silver-mordenite for radiologic gas capture from complex streams: Dual catalytic CH3I decomposition and I confinement
abstract: (250 words) The effective capture and storage of radiological iodine (129I) remains a strong concern for safe nuclear waste storage and safe nuclear energy. Silver-containing mordenite (MOR) is a longstanding benchmark for iodine capture. In nuclear fuel reprocessing scenarios, complex gas streams will be present and the need for high selectivity of all iodine containing compounds is of the utmost importance for safety and the environment. In particular, a molecular level understanding of the sorption of organic iodine compounds is not well understood. Here we probe the structure and distribution of methyl iodide sorbed by silver-containing MOR using a combination of crystallographic and materials characterization techniques including: infrared spectroscopy, thermogravimetric analysis with mass spectrometry, Micro-X-ray Fluorescence, powder X-ray diffraction analysis, and pair distribution function analysis. The iodine is captured inside the MOR pore in the form of AgI nanoparticles, that is consistent with the pores sizes of the MOR, indicating that the molecule is both physically and chemically captured in the Ag-MOR. The organic component is surface catalyzed by the zeolite via the formation of Surface Methoxy Species (SMS) that result in downstream organics of dimethyl ether and methanol formation.
text:
Silver-Mordenite for Radiologic Gas Capture from 
Complex Streams: �Dual Catalytic CH3I 
Decomposition and I Confinement
Tina M. Nenoff,*,^ Mark A. Rodriguez&, Nick Soelberg+, 
Karena W. Chapman#,^

Nanoscale Science Department, Sandia National Laboratories, Albuquerque,

New Mexico 87185, United States
+ Idaho National Laboratory, 2525 Fremont Ave, Idaho Falls, ID 83402
& Materials Characterization and Performance Department, Sandia National Laboratories, 
Albuquerque, New Mexico 87185, United States
# X-ray Science Division, Advanced Photon Source, Argonne National Laboratory, 
Argonne, Illinois 60439, United States
^ Corresponding authors:
tmnenof@sandia.gov; 505-844-0340; Sandia National Laboratories, PO Box 5800, MS 
1415, Albuquerque, NM 87185-1415
chapmank@anl.gov; 630-252-0127; Argonne National Laboratory, 9700 S Cass Ave, 
Lemont, IL, 60439
Highlights (3-5 needed)
• CH3I is catalytically cleaved over Ag°-Mordenite zeolite
• Surface Methoxy Species (SMS) are formed at the Mordenite acid sites 
• Iodine is captured by the Ag° to form pore occluded AgI
• NO is co-adsorbed by the Mordenite
• SMS combine to form methanol, dimethyl ether and methyl nitrates which are captured 
downstream in addition to excess iodine species (HI, I2)
SAND2014-1157J

Abstract (250 words)
The effective capture and storage of radiological iodine (129I) remains a strong concern 
for safe nuclear waste storage and safe nuclear energy.  Silver-containing mordenite 
(MOR) is a longstanding benchmark for iodine capture.  In nuclear fuel reprocessing 
scenarios, complex gas streams will be present and the need for high selectivity of all 
iodine containing compounds is of the utmost importance for safety and the environment.  
In particular, a molecular level understanding of the sorption of organic iodine 
compounds is not well understood. Here we probe the structure and distribution of methyl 
iodide sorbed by silver-containing MOR using a combination of crystallographic and 
materials characterization techniques including: infrared spectroscopy, thermogravimetric 
analysis with mass spectrometry, Micro-X-ray Fluorescence, powder X-ray diffraction 
analysis, and pair distribution function analysis. The iodine is captured inside the MOR 
pore in the form of AgI nanoparticles, that is consistent with the pores sizes of the MOR, 
indicating that the molecule is both physically and chemically captured in the Ag-MOR. 
The organic component is surface catalyzed by the zeolite via the formation of Surface 
Methoxy Species (SMS) that result in downstream organics of dimethyl ether and 
methanol formation.
Keywords.
Mordenite, Methyl Iodide, Synchrotron, Pair Distribution Function (PDF), Fission gas 
capture

1. Introduction
Safe nuclear energy is inevitably linked to the safe storage of nuclear waste. As such, 
extreme selectivity and durability are critical to materials used in nuclear separations and 
long-term waste storage. Numerous radionuclides with varying radiological decay half-
lives have been identified as “volatile radionuclides” including 129I , 3H, 14C, and noble 
gases (most notably isotopes of Kr and Xe). The effective separation, capture and storage 
of these radionuclides are areas of active research.[1] 129I is one of the radionucleotides 
that remains of strong interest and concern. While this radionucleotide is only found in 
small concentrations in nuclear effluent, the effective capture and storage of iodine
(found in aqueous streams as H129I, 129I2, and/or organic iodides) is critically important to 
public safety due to its involvement in human metabolic processes and its long half-life 
(~107 years).[2] The leading approach to capture radioactive gaseous by-products such as 
iodine during nuclear fuel reprocessing involves sorption onto silver-containing zeolites.
Indeed, for many decades the silver-containing zeolite mordenite (MOR, Figure 1) 
has been a benchmark for radiological iodine capture. Until recently, the molecular basis 
for its performance had remained largely unexplored.[3, 4] We have successfully 
undertaken structure-property studies into understanding the structure of Ag-MOR,[5] as 
well as the capture of I2 gas by a variety of porous crystalline materials (including 
zeolites and Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs),[6-9] the storage of the iodine gas in 
durable waste forms[10, 11], and the combined storage/capture of iodate and iodides 
from caustic aqueous streams[12]. Worldwide, a larger effort for the capture and storage 
of iodine is underway. Additional capture strategies include I2 gas with silver-
impregnated amorphous silica[13], chalcogel-based aerogels [14, 15], and MOFs for the 
extraction of I2 from organic solutions [16]; additional storage strategies include the 
incorporation of iodine in structurally compatible apatite mineral analogs[17, 18] and 
iodine immobilization via hot isostatic pressing.[19]
Because of the competitive adsorption encountered by sorption materials in complex 
off-gas streams, molecular studies are being extended to the interaction of silver-
mordenite with other iodine-containing components. In particular, we extend our studies 
to the sorption of iodine from methyl iodide (CH3-I) by Ag-MOR. In addition to its 129I 
isotope being a radiological by-product of nuclear fuel reprocessing,[20] methyl iodide is 

known as a pesticide[21] and a chemical methylating reagent,[22] including in the
methanol-to-gasoline (MTG) catalytic process.[23] These processes benefit from the 
ability to understand the structure-property relationship between CH3-I and its adsorption 
properties.
To rationally optimize the capture process, the subsequent processing for long term 
storage and, ultimately, to develop the next generation of iodine capture materials, it is 
first important to understand the distribution and properties of iodide formed (AgI) and 
the role of the zeolite support.
Here we present a differential pair distribution function (d-PDF) study of CH3-I-
treated silver-containing MOR to probe the structure and distribution of the supported 
AgI—the organic iodine-capture vehicle. The pair distribution function (PDF) method 
probes the local structure of nano-scale and amorphous materials, where conventional 
Bragg crystallographic analysis yields only limited insight. It provides the distribution of 
atomic distances within a material, and while not intrinsically chemically specific, by 
using a differential approach to PDF analysis[24] it is possible to isolate contributions 
from individual components within a material.[25] This involves subtracting a reference 
PDF, measured for the host zeolite, from the iodine-loaded system, such that atomic 
distances from the zeolite can be separated from those in the supported material. It is a 
standard practice to reduce the Ag+-exchanged MOR prior to use. Here, the capture of 
CH3-I from a complex acidic stream by a reduced silver MOR is investigated.
Significant complexity of the organic iodide (guest) loaded Ag-MOR (host) results 
from the inherent challenge in decoupling the guest structure and framework distortion
from the host lattice. Therefore, in order to develop an understanding of the 
structure−function relationship, a combined experimental approach was necessary. This 
includes infrared (IR) spectroscopy, thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) with mass 
spectrometry (MS), Micro-X-ray Fluorescence (μ-XRF), powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) 
analysis, and pair distribution function (PDF) analysis.
2. Experimental

2.1 CH3-I loaded Ag°-MOR. Ag°-MOR was provided by Oak Ridge National 
Laboratory (ORNL). IONEX Ag-MOR (AW-300) is a silver exchanged natural 
mordenite. A sample with reduced silver metal was prepared by heating at 150°C in 
flowing 3% H2 for approximately 12 hours, resulting in a material with a standard 
formula of 
(Ag,H*)1.52Fe0.06Ca0.11K0.16Al2.35Si9.47O24•4H2O, H* = charge balancing 
proton adsorbed from the reducing steam.[26, 27]
Static Adsorption Experiments. A ceramic boat holding Ag°-MOR (natural) is 
placed in a tube furnace. A CH3-I vapor in a N2 carrier gas, flowing at ~ 2 ml/min, is 
streamed over the zeolite for 24 hours at 150°C. Excess nonadsorbed CH3-I was captured 
in a water bath outside the tube furnace. This resulted in a loading of 8.5 wt% I on the 
Ag-MOR. The resultant loaded MOR sample lightened in color from dark gray to 
yellowish-gray.
Deep Bed Iodine Sorbent Test Experiments. (Figure 2) Approximately 10g of the 
Ag°-MOR was loaded into a vertical sorption bed column (1.9 cm diameter, 3.8 cm
length) that was located in a temperature controlled oven. The inlet mixed gas stream 
approximated that consisted of 1.8% water vapor, 800 ppm NO, 800 ppm NO2, and 57 
ppm CH3I in a carrier stream of air, with a flow rate of 0.73 L/min and at 150°C.[28]  
Sorption experiment time was approximately 1 month. Post-sorption samples for 
structural analysis were taken from 1.25 to 5 cm bed depth.
2.2 High-energy synchrotron X-ray scattering measurements. High energy X-ray 
scattering data were collected at beamline 11-ID-B at the Advanced Photon Source at 
Argonne National Laboratory for sample recovered from the online flow sorption 
experiments, loaded in polyimide capillaries. Total scattering data suitable for PDF 
analysis were collected at ~87 keV, λ = 0.1430 Å for the sample recovered from the 
sorption experiment and at ~90 keV, λ = 0.1370 Å for unreduced Ag+-MOR (for the 
reference PDF) and the pristine / unexposed Ag°-MOR sample used for sorption 
experiment. A large amorphous silicon-based area detector was used to record two-
dimensional diffraction images.[29, 30] These data were reduced to one-dimensional 
scattering data within fit2d.[31]
2.3 Rietveld structural analysis. To model the X-ray diffraction data the atomic 
coordinates of the framework atoms were taken from previous reports. Refinements are 

carried out in GSAS. Initial refinements of the XRD patterns entailed modeling of the 
atom coordinates for the Al-Si-O framework only, with no attempt to refine site positions 
and occupancies of the species within the large cavities of the zeolite.
These initial 
refinements on the Al-Si-O cage included refinement of variables for lattice parameters, 
along with the scale factor, peak shape parameters, and background. Upon completion of 
this initial fit, Difference-Fourier maps were employed to visualize the unmodeled 
electron density within the pore cavities. Locations of candidate sites for Ag, water, 
iodine, and other possible species were established based on scattering magnitude and 
general coordination to the zeolite framework. Final refinements included site occupancy 
and positional parameters for the species present in the pore with the site occupancies 
restrained to be consistent with known elemental composition determination from X-ray 
Fluorescence (XRF) results. Framework atoms were modeled with a Biso (isotropic 
temperature) value of ~1.0 Å2. Atom species present in the pores were modeled with Biso
of 4.0 Å2.
2.4 Pair Distribution Function (PDF) analysis. The PDFs, G(r), were obtained 
from the total scattering data, collected to high values of momentum transfer, Qmax = 20 
Å−1, using PDFgetX2.[32] Contributions from the background, Compton scattering, 
fluorescence, to the total scattering data were subtracted as described previously.[29]
Differential PDFs (d-PDFs) which isolate the correlations involving the species within 
the zeolite pores were recovered by subtracting the PDF for the unreduced Ag+-MOR as 
described previously.[6] Structural models were refined against the d-PDFs within 
PDFgui.[33] The intensities of selected peaks were quantified by fitting Gaussian 
functions within fityk.[34]
2.5 Thermogravimetric-Differential Scanning Calorimetry (TGA-DSC) and 
Mass Spectrometry (MS) analyses. The thermal desorption of guests and eventual 
framework decomposition was investigated by TGA-DSC-MS. Measurements used a 
SDTQ600 TA instrument, equipped with a mass-spectrometer gas analyzer MS-
ThermoStarTM from Pfeiffer Vacuum. Samples were heated at 10°C/min to 800°C under 
nitrogen flow.
2.6 Micro-X-ray fluorescence.  The µ-XRF analysis was performed using a Bruker 
M4 Tornado µ-XRF mapping system.  The instrument was equipped with a micro-

focused Rh source (50 kV, 600 µA) with a poly-capillary optic (~30 µm spot-size). 
Fluorescence spectra were collected using two silicon-drift detectors. Powder specimens 
were loaded in small Teflon cups (~2 mm inner diameter), then secured to the 
xy-translation stage within the M4, and XRF spectra were collected under vacuum (~10-3
Torr). Quantification of the elements (Si, Al, Ag, I, Ca, Mn, and Ca) was possible using
the “Quantitative analysis” routines within the M4 software.  As the data is uncalibrated, 
only approximate elemental quantities are available.
2.7 Gas Chromatography – Flame Ionization Detector (GC-FID). Gaseous methyl 
iodide concentrations are measured using a gas chromatograph (GC; Hewlett Packard 
5890 GC) with a flame ionization detector (FID), and a solid-phase micro-extractor 
(SPME) to improve the GCFID detection limit to about 10 ppb for methyl iodide. Gas 
samples can be injected into the GC using a sample loop, syringes, or solid-phase micro-
extraction (SPME) syringes, which improve the detection limit by approximately 100x.
2.8 Infrared Spectroscopy (IR).  Data are collected on a Thermo Scientific Nicolet 
iS10 Smart iTR. Samples are sub-milligram in size. Each sample data set is collected in 
air and in the range of 3500 - 500 cm-1.
3. Results
Mordenite, (Ca,Na2,K2)Al2Si10O24·7H2O, is the zeolite-of-choice for radioactive 
iodine capture due to its high Si:Al ratio which enhances stability in the acidic waste 
streams and the relatively high Ag-loadings possible. The MOR framework structure 
defines one-dimensional channels (12-rings, 6.5 × 7.0 Å) parallel to the crystallographic 
c-axis which contain the charge balancing cations.
Based on weight gained during the deep bed sorption testing, iodine loading onto the 
Ag°-MOR is approximately 5.2 wt%; this translates into approximately 55% utilization
of silver loaded onto the MOR zeolite.  However, it is important to note that some of this 
weight gain may be from adsorbed organic components from interaction of stream gases 
with the zeolites (e.g., CH3I, H2O, or NOx).  Inorganic iodine (either I2 or HI) is detected 
downstream of the sorbent beds after column breakthrough occurs, indicating that methyl 
iodide was being converted into other compounds when in contact with, or through 
reaction with, the Ag-MOR.  Consistent with prior test results,[35, 36] after breakthrough 

of the sorbent occurs, a significant portion (from approximately 10 to 90+%, depending 
on degree of breakthrough) of the iodine that passes through the bed is not methyl iodide, 
but is either I2 or HI. The stoichiometric formula calculated from ICP-MS for the Ag°-
MOR 
(natural) 
sample 
that 
is 
exposed 
to 
CH3-I
is: 
(AgI)1.02
(H1.44K0.16Ca0.10Fe0.05Al2.53Si9.63O24) with residual Ag = 0.42.  Note, this is a bulk 
calculation of an inhomogeneous material.
Various organic compounds are detected in the sorption bed gas outlet effluent 
stream. They include Dimethyl Ether (DME), methyl nitrate, and methanol. These results 
are generally consistent with similar prior research:  Haefner[35] observes methanol in 
the off-gas and suggests it is a byproduct of methyl iodide adsorption on Ag-MOR, while  
Scheele[36] proposes that the observed methanol and DME in the off-gas are organic 
byproducts from methyl iodide adsorption on Ag-MOR.
Sorption onto the Ag°-MOR proceeds at a relatively high level (~ 50+ %) of the Ag 
utilization and saturation level for the sorbent. When approaching column breakthrough, 
the sorption process slows, and the outlet effluent gas composition approaches the same 
composition as the inlet gas, with the addition of the new organic compounds including 
DME and methanol. A quantitative analysis of the organic byproducts in the sorbent bed 
outlet gas suggests that not all of the organic moiety (CH3
+) from the methyl iodide is 
quantitatively represented by the amounts of the organic byproducts in the outgas effluent 
gas.  This result suggests that some of the organic moiety is also adsorbed on the sorbent, 
or else is converted into other gaseous byproducts – such compounds as CO, CO2, H2O 
and/or H2.  
Refinement of structural models against the d-PDF of Ag°-MOR, prior to CH3I 
exposure, indicates that it consists of 8 nm Ag nanoparticles with a face-centered cubic 
structure (a = 4.078 Å, R ~ 13.4%) supported on MOR. (Figure 5)  By contrast, the d-
PDF for the post-sorption, CH3I-treated system was dominated by features at short 
distance, below ca. 6 Å. Refinements of models against the data within PDFgui, 
suggested that these were predominately subnanometer clusters of an AgI phase (likely α-
AgI) with a minor component of larger γ-AgI nanoparticles (11%, ca. 6 nm). The largest 
deviation in the fit of the AgI phases to the data is apparent for the feature(s) at 2.7 - 3.0 
Å which corresponds to the nearest-neighbor Ag-I distance. While the overall intensity of 

this feature is well modeled by the AgI phase, it appears to contain contributions from 
two distinct peaks with similar distances. These peaks are centered at 2.77 Å and 2.97 Å, 
with relative peak areas of 3:1. These distinct distances may reflect local 
defects/distortions in the α-AgI phase.
There was no evidence for residual face-centered cubic metallic Ag°, suggesting that 
either the Ag° has been fully reacted to form AgI or that the Ag° is completely re-oxidized 
during the on-stream sorption process. Given the distribution of the subnanometer AgI 
clusters versus larger AgI nanoparticles observed here, and our previous studies that 
indicate that subnanometer AgI clusters are the more heavily product when iodine (albeit 
as I2, not CH3I) is captured by unreduced Ag+-MOR, we propose that a degree of 
reduction of the supported Ag° metal to Ag+ ion likely occurs in-situ/online. 
The thermal analysis TGA-DSC-MS data (see Figures 3 and 4) provides a great deal 
of information about the adsorbed molecules and compounds, plus the stability of the 
captured iodine.  In the TGA, ~0.5 wt% weight loss around 100°C and ~2.5 wt% loss 
centered around ~150°C is associated with CO2 loss. In the DSC, an endothermic peak at 
152°C is associated with the to AgI temperature-induced phase transition in Ag-
MOR[6] (the ambient two-phase mixture of - and γ-polymorphs transforms to the 
(super) ion-conducting -phase above 147°C).[37-40] Furthermore, there is a general 
exothermic slope matching the TGA weight loss seen at ~ 225°C. Clarification of the 
thermal events is available through the comparison with the coincidental MS data. Below 
100°C, there is CO2 loss that is presumably the combustion product of the excess organic 
at the bulk surface of the zeolites. Near 160°C, a combination of CO2, H2O and possibly 
–OH are released; this corresponds to occluded and/or pore adsorbed water and organic 
molecules (the CO2 is again a product combustion). It is at approximately 225°C that the 
iodine (I) and CO begin their release, in addition to maximum CO2 loss. It is important to 
note that the O2 and CO rise (release) until the maximum temperature of 600°C, 
suggesting that they are continuously off gassing due to heating above 225°C. There is no 
evidence of AgI, NO2, I2 loss in the temperature range studied. 
As expected, the spot XRF analysis of the CH3I-Ag-MOR loaded samples detect Ag, 
Ca and Fe, with Ca and Fe in lower molar fractions than Ag. (Figure S1) This is 
confirmation of incomplete ion exchange of the silver for the natural mordenite charge 

balancing cations. (Figure S2). This data confirms that no AgI is lost to volatilization 
above its melting point of 556°C, but instead shows the retention of the AgI nanoparticles 
inside the MOR pores. This information combined with the lack of AgI melting in the 
TGA/DSC is confirmation that the in-pore confinement of these nanoparticles adds to 
their stability, and allows for the probably “amorphization”[41] and retention of the AgI
at higher temperatures. 
Comparison of the as-received Ag°-MOR and the post-exposure to CH3I-Ag°-MOR 
(see Figure S3) IR spectra reveal no difference in the traces. This is further evidence that 
there is neither permanent organic functionalization of the zeolite framework, nor is there 
any capture of CH3I in the pores of the MOR (as a possible functionalized silver 
nanoparticle).  This data is confirmation of that elucidated from the crystallographic 
analyses.  
4. Discussion
This set of experiments was preformed under simulated industrial conditions of 
fission off-gas capture for nuclear fuel reprocessing. The zeolites are natural and as a 
result have complex pore cation distributions. Furthermore, they are in bulk pelletized 
form throughout the study, including both when reduced and when packed into the 
sorption beds. 
The sorption of iodine from CH3I by Ag-MOR is possible through a series of catalytic 
and sorption processes. Through bulk elemental analysis of the silver loaded natural 
mordenite, we observe that the charge balancing cations are Ca, K and presumably H (to 
compensate for the reduced Ag+ to Ag° in the pores). Therefore it is reasonable to assume 
that on a localized scale, there were regions of the zeolite that were basic in nature (Ag+, 
Ca2+, K+) and areas that were acidic in nature (Ag° and H+ in the pores).
Much research has been performed into the catalytic nature of zeolites that are 
exposed to methyl halides and further the reactivity of surface alkoxy species on zeolites 
surfaces for the initiation mechanisms of the methanol-to-gasoline (MTG) catalytic 
process. Their insights are valuable in understanding the methodology of iodine capture 
of CH3I by Ag-MOR in complex streams.

In the case of basic zeolites used in the conversion of methyl halides, Haw, et.al.,[42]
utilized 13C magic angle spinning (MAS) NMR to study the formation of intermediate 
surface products on the zeolite including methyl halides converting to surface methoxy 
group intermediate on route to the formation of ethylene.  Furthermore, he showed that 
depending on the level of basicity of the zeolite (as determined by the charge balancing 
cations and/or zeolite acidity level), methyl iodide conversion to hydrocarbons reactions 
commenced at 225°C over CsX zeolite.[43]
In the case of acidic zeolites, Wang, et.al., found similar intermediate species 
formations (surface methoxy species, SMS; example within Figure 6) via 13C MAS NMR 
studies. The SMS act as methylating agents on acidic zeolites when they are reacted with 
a number of molecules including methanol and hydrochlorides. The SMS can then react 
with an number of molecules in the stream to form various hydrocarbon products. 
Examples include (1) the SMS reacts with methanol to form dimethyl ether (DME) plus
an acidic site on the zeolite; or the SMS reacts with water to form methanol (CH3OH) 
plus and acidic site on the zeolite. Wang further showed that SMS on acidic zeolite 
surfaces produced CO and CO2 via the oxidation of the SMS in the presence of 
oxygen.[44]
Based on the data collected on the molecules collected downstream from the CH3I 
adsorption over the Ag-MOR, the TGA/DSC-MS of the iodine loaded AgI-MOR and the 
structural analyses, we postulate the following reactions occur in the process of capturing 
the iodine from the complex input stream of CH3I, H2O, NO, NO2 and air.   First, CH3I is 
cleaved to form an iodine species and a surface methoxy species at the low operating 
temperature of the adsorption process, 150°C. Second, the iodine reacts with the Ag to 
form AgI nanoparticles inside the zeolite pore. Third, the SMS react with water to form 
methanol, and then further react with methanol to form dimethyl ether. Both methanol 
and dimethyl ether are collected downstream of the adsorption column. This is pictorially 
described in Figure 6.
The TGA-DSC-MS data of the final iodine adsorbed zeolite indicates that at 225°C, 
there is a release of iodine, NO, CO and CO2 species from the sample.  The NO is
probably desorbing from the zeolite pore as they are readily adsorbed by acidified 
aluminosilicate zeolites, including MOR.[45]  CO and CO2 are possibly produced by the 

oxidation of excess and/or unreacted SMS, that are on the zeolite surface, by either 
sorbed H2O or nitrates. There is no evidence of AgI loss (melting point 556°C) up to 
600°C. Instead, as seen in the literature, it appears that the AgI is forming an 
“amorphous”[41] phase that continues to reside in the zeolite pores.  This has been 
confirmed by the XRF data that identifies Ag and I in similar ratios in the pre- and post-
heated (600°C) CH3I-Ag-MOR samples. 
Interestingly, the resultant AgI-MOR product after CH3I interactions, appears to be a 
less stable iodine capture material that that formed from the adsorption of pure I2 gas on 
same as-received starting material Ag°-MOR (natural) to form AgI-MOR (I loss begins 
at approximately 360°C). (See figure S4) Examination of the data leads to the conclusion 
that the desorption of NO and CO may be facilitating the loss of excess I (acidified, and 
in the form of HI) from the pores and bulk surface of the mordenite.
5. Conclusions
The capture mechanism of radiological fission gases is of vital interest to the 
population and the environment. Through a combination of materials chemistry, complex 
gas stream deep bed adsorption studies, and crystallographic (X-ray diffraction 
refinement and Pair Distribution Function analysis) methods, the mechanism of iodine 
adsorption from methyl iodide in an acidic wet stream is elucidated.  
The CH3I is catalytically cleaved over the partially reduced Ag°-mordenite natural 
zeolite.  In that cleaving process, surface methoxy species are formed at the mordenite 
acid sites; the iodine species is either captured by the silver to form occluded AgI in the 
MOR pores, or it reacts to form unbound I2 or HI.  There is evidence of nitrite species 
also being adsorbed from the inlet gas stream on the mordenite.  As the reaction 
proceeds, the SMS react to form a number of off-gas species such as methanol, dimethyl 
ether, and methyl nitrate.
The combination of chemical, thermal and mechanical stability of the zeolites make 
them strong candidates for the use in nuclear energy applications. The chemical and 
physical confinement of the radiological species (e.g., Ag129I) inside the pores of the 
zeolite allows for the safe manipulation of the material into a waste form for long-term 
decay storage. Research into the incorporation of iodine captured from complex streams 

into waste forms that are chemically durable (e.g., low temperature sintering Bi-Si oxide 
glass)[10] for a variety of postulated repository geologies is ongoing.

Figure Captions
Figure 1. The MOR structure. The MOR framework defines one-dimensional channels 
(12-rings, 6.5×7.0 Å) parallel to the c-axis which contain the exchangeable cations and 
water molecules (omitted for clarity);[46] standard formula: (X1+)2AlSi10O24.
Figure 2. Iodine sorbent deep bed test system used in the capture of iodine from a 
complex stream containing CH3I, H2O, NO and NO2 with a carrier gas of air.
Figure 3. Raw PDFs of CH3I-Ag-MOR (blue) and pristine Ag-MOR (red), which both 
include the zeolite component. Differential shown in green. PDF shows a standard AgI 
phase [6] with sub 6 Å particle size: one well defined peak at 2.8Å and a broad feature 
between 4.5 and 5 Å. 
Figure 4. TGA-DSC of CH3I-Ag-MOR sample in flowing He.  Dip at 152°C represents 
the endothermic, temperature induced, phase transition of the pore occluded /- to -
AgI nanoparticles.
Figure 5. MS data from simultaneous TGA-DSC-MS, run under flowing He.
Figure 6. A schematic portraying the dual catalytic CH3I decomposition and I 
confinement reaction pathway over the Ag°-MOR zeolite. The SMS unit is identified.

Supplementary Data
Figure S1. Example of one -XRF spot analysis of CH3I-Ag-MOR as-received. A ratio 
of 1 Ag : 2.4 I is approximated in the sample. 

Figure S2. Example of one -XRF of CH3I-Ag-MOR, after heating to 600°C. There is 
little to no change in the approximated ratio of Ag:I in the CH3I-Ag-MOR sample after 
heating to 600°C (~ 1 Ag : 2.25 I).

Figure S3. IR spectra of pristine (Ag°-MOR (natural), as received) as compared to the 
post exposure to CH3I containing gas stream. There is no evidence of methyl groups 
permanently attached to the AgI-MOR structure.

Figure S4.  TGA-DSC-MS of AgI-MOR (natural, 8.7 wt% Iodine loaded) provided by S. 
Bruffey and R.T. Jubin, ORNL.
